title: Sockets & Ports
description: The programming interface for network communication — a socket is an endpoint identified by IP address + port number.
fullExplanation: A socket is an endpoint for network communication, identified by a 5-tuple: (protocol, source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port). Applications use the socket API (BSD sockets) to create connections, send, and receive data. The OS manages the socket and the underlying TCP/UDP connection. Port numbers identify which application should receive incoming data — well-known ports (0-1023) are reserved for standard services.

portRanges.name: Well-Known Ports
portRanges.description: Reserved for standard services. Require root/admin to bind. HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, SSH=22.

portRanges.name: Registered Ports
portRanges.description: Assigned by IANA for specific applications. MySQL=3306, PostgreSQL=5432, RDP=3389.

portRanges.name: Dynamic/Ephemeral
portRanges.description: Assigned by OS for client-side connections. Randomly chosen per connection.

socketStates.description: Client sent SYN, waiting for SYN-ACK
Server received SYN, sent SYN-ACK, waiting for ACK
Received ACK for FIN, waiting for remote FIN
Both FINs exchanged. Wait 2×MSL before closing (prevents stale packets).
Received FIN, waiting for application to close

keyFacts: TIME_WAIT: 2×MSL = 2×60s = 120s — prevents old packets from corrupting new connections
SO_REUSEADDR: socket option to reuse port in TIME_WAIT — needed for fast server restarts
netstat -an / ss -tn: list all sockets and their states
File descriptor: each socket is a file descriptor in Unix — 'everything is a file'
